_meta.source: MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise (https://attack.mitre.org). Hand-curated, concise summaries authored offline; not a verbatim copy of the STIX bundle.
_meta.note: Curated subset covering exactly the technique IDs recognized in src/mitre/matrix.py (the SOC-relevant set). Fields are deliberately short (token budget) and intentionally general where a verbatim ATT&CK phrasing was uncertain — accuracy over completeness. No network access is used to build or refresh this asset.
_meta.license: ATT&CK content (c) The MITRE Corporation, reused under the ATT&CK Terms of Use.

T1003.name: OS Credential Dumping
T1003.description: Adversaries dump credential material (password hashes, plaintext passwords, Kerberos tickets) from the operating system, such as from LSASS memory, the SAM database, or /etc/shadow, to enable lateral movement and privileged access.
T1003.detection: Monitor for processes reading LSASS memory, access to the SAM/SECURITY registry hives or /etc/shadow, and known tools (Mimikatz, procdump, comsvcs.dll MiniDump). Correlate unexpected credential-store access with the accessing process lineage.
T1003.mitigation: Enable Credential Guard/LSA protection, restrict local admin rights, disable WDigest plaintext caching, and apply least privilege so credential stores are not broadly readable.

T1005.name: Data from Local System
T1005.description: Adversaries search local file systems and directories on a compromised host to find and collect files of interest prior to exfiltration.
T1005.detection: Look for unusual command-line file discovery/collection activity, mass file reads by a single process, and staging of archives. Baseline normal file-access patterns per user and host.
T1005.mitigation: Data loss prevention, restricting access to sensitive files with least privilege, and monitoring/auditing access to high-value file shares reduce exposure.

T1016.name: System Network Configuration Discovery
T1016.description: Adversaries enumerate the host's network configuration and settings (IP addresses, routes, DNS, ARP tables, proxy settings) to orient themselves and plan further movement.
T1016.detection: Monitor for execution of utilities such as ipconfig/ifconfig, route, arp, nbtstat, and netsh, especially in rapid succession or from unusual parent processes.
T1016.mitigation: This is discovery of built-in system information and is difficult to prevent directly; focus on detection and on limiting the value of the information via network segmentation.

T1021.name: Remote Services
T1021.description: Adversaries use valid accounts to log into remote services such as SSH, RDP, SMB/admin shares, VNC, or WinRM to move laterally between systems.
T1021.detection: Monitor authentication logs for remote logons using valid accounts from unexpected sources, unusual internal service connections, and new or atypical remote-session activity.
T1021.mitigation: Enforce MFA, restrict which accounts can use remote services, disable unnecessary services, and segment the network to limit reachable systems.

T1027.name: Obfuscated Files or Information
T1027.description: Adversaries make files or command content hard to discover or analyze by encoding, encrypting, packing, or otherwise obfuscating payloads and scripts to evade defenses.
T1027.detection: Flag encoded command-line arguments (e.g. base64 PowerShell -enc), high-entropy files, packed binaries, and script deobfuscation-then-execute patterns. Use script block logging.
T1027.mitigation: Prevention is difficult; use antivirus/AMSI, behavioral detection, and script logging. Application control can block unknown or packed executables.

T1029.name: Scheduled Transfer
T1029.description: Adversaries schedule data exfiltration to occur only at certain times of day or intervals (e.g. to blend with normal business traffic), rather than continuously.
T1029.detection: Look for periodic, regular outbound data transfers to the same destination, especially outside business hours, and correlate with prior collection/staging activity.
T1029.mitigation: Network intrusion prevention and egress filtering that inspect and constrain outbound connections can limit scheduled exfiltration channels.

T1036.name: Masquerading
T1036.description: Adversaries manipulate the name, location, or metadata of files/processes to make malicious artifacts appear legitimate (e.g. naming malware after a trusted system binary).
T1036.detection: Compare process names against their true image paths and signatures, flag legitimate names running from unusual directories, and detect mismatched file extensions or spoofed signatures.
T1036.mitigation: Application control and code signing enforcement help ensure only legitimate, correctly-located binaries execute.

T1039.name: Data from Network Shared Drive
T1039.description: Adversaries search network shares and remote file systems accessible from a compromised host to locate and collect files of interest before exfiltration.
T1039.detection: Monitor for enumeration and bulk reads of network shares by a single account or host, and unusual access to file servers outside a user's normal pattern.
T1039.mitigation: Apply least-privilege share permissions, audit access to sensitive shares, and use data loss prevention on high-value repositories.

T1041.name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
T1041.description: Adversaries steal data by sending it back over the same command-and-control channel used to control the compromised host, blending exfiltration with existing C2 traffic.
T1041.detection: Look for larger-than-expected outbound data on established C2 sessions, unusual upload/download ratios, and beaconing connections carrying bulk data.
T1041.mitigation: Network intrusion prevention, egress filtering, and detection of C2 channels themselves are the primary controls; segment sensitive data away from internet-facing hosts.

T1046.name: Network Service Discovery
T1046.description: Adversaries scan hosts and networks to enumerate listening services and open ports, identifying targets and vulnerabilities for exploitation or lateral movement.
T1046.detection: Detect port-scanning behavior — many connection attempts to sequential ports or many hosts from one source in a short window — via network sensors and firewall logs.
T1046.mitigation: Network segmentation, disabling unnecessary services, and intrusion prevention limit the reachable attack surface and detect scanning.

T1047.name: Windows Management Instrumentation
T1047.description: Adversaries abuse Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to execute commands and payloads locally or on remote hosts, and to query system information.
T1047.detection: Monitor for wmic.exe usage, WmiPrvSE.exe spawning shells/processes, and WMI-based remote execution. Enable WMI activity logging and correlate with lateral-movement patterns.
T1047.mitigation: Restrict WMI remote access to authorized administrators, apply least privilege, and use application control to limit what WMI can launch.

T1048.name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
T1048.description: Adversaries exfiltrate data over a protocol different from the main C2 channel, such as DNS, ICMP, FTP, or SMTP, to evade monitoring focused on the primary channel.
T1048.detection: Look for anomalous data volumes over DNS/ICMP/SMTP, unusual protocol usage from a host, and long or high-entropy DNS queries indicative of tunneling.
T1048.mitigation: Egress filtering, restricting protocols allowed outbound, and network intrusion prevention that inspects DNS/ICMP reduce alternative exfiltration paths.

T1053.name: Scheduled Task/Job
T1053.description: Adversaries abuse task scheduling utilities (Windows Task Scheduler/schtasks, cron, at) to execute malicious code, often for persistence or for running with elevated privileges.
T1053.detection: Monitor for creation or modification of scheduled tasks and cron jobs, especially those launching scripts/binaries from unusual paths, and correlate with the creating account.
T1053.mitigation: Restrict who can create scheduled tasks, apply least privilege, and audit task/cron changes. Configure task creation to require elevated permissions.

T1056.name: Input Capture
T1056.description: Adversaries capture user input — via keylogging, credential-prompt spoofing, or API hooking — to steal credentials and other sensitive information.
T1056.detection: Look for processes registering keyboard hooks or GUI credential prompts, unexpected drivers, and unusual access to input APIs. Monitor for keylogger artifacts.
T1056.mitigation: Endpoint protection, application control, and MFA (to reduce the value of captured passwords) mitigate input capture.

T1057.name: Process Discovery
T1057.description: Adversaries enumerate running processes on a host to understand the environment, find security tooling, and identify targets for injection or termination.
T1057.detection: Monitor for execution of tasklist, ps, Get-Process, and similar, particularly from scripts or unusual parents and in bursts alongside other discovery commands.
T1057.mitigation: Built-in discovery of process information is hard to prevent; focus on detecting anomalous or scripted enumeration.

T1059.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
T1059.description: Adversaries abuse command and script interpreters (PowerShell, cmd, Bash, Python, Windows Script Host) to execute commands, scripts, and payloads.
T1059.detection: Enable command-line and script-block logging; flag suspicious interpreter invocations, encoded commands, and interpreters spawned by document/office or web-server processes.
T1059.mitigation: Constrain or disable unneeded interpreters, use PowerShell Constrained Language Mode and AMSI, and apply application control to restrict script execution.

T1068.name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
T1068.description: Adversaries exploit software vulnerabilities in the OS kernel, drivers, or privileged services to elevate their privileges on a compromised host.
T1068.detection: Look for process crashes and anomalous child processes of privileged services, unexpected SYSTEM/root shells, and known exploit indicators. Correlate with unpatched vulnerabilities.
T1068.mitigation: Prompt patching, exploit protection (DEP/ASLR/CFG), least privilege, and application isolation/sandboxing reduce exploitable surface.

T1070.name: Indicator Removal
T1070.description: Adversaries delete or alter evidence of their activity — clearing event logs, deleting files, removing command history, or manipulating timestamps — to hinder detection and forensics.
T1070.detection: Alert on event-log clearing (e.g. Windows Event ID 1102), history file truncation, mass file deletion, and use of tools like wevtutil or 'log clear' commands.
T1070.mitigation: Forward logs off-host to a central SIEM, restrict permissions to delete logs, and enable tamper protection so local removal does not erase the record.

T1071.name: Application Layer Protocol
T1071.description: Adversaries use standard application-layer protocols (HTTP/S, DNS, mail) for command and control, blending malicious traffic with legitimate protocol usage.
T1071.detection: Look for beaconing patterns, unusual user-agents/JA3 fingerprints, connections to newly-seen or low-reputation domains, and protocol content anomalies.
T1071.mitigation: Network intrusion prevention, TLS inspection where permitted, and egress filtering to known-good destinations reduce covert C2 channels.

T1074.name: Data Staged
T1074.description: Adversaries collect and store data in a central location or archive on the host or network prior to exfiltration, often compressing or encrypting it first.
T1074.detection: Look for creation of large archives in temp/staging directories, unusual write volumes to a single location, and archive utilities (zip, rar, tar) run by non-standard users.
T1074.mitigation: Monitoring for large file writes and archive creation in unusual locations, plus data loss prevention, helps catch staging before exfiltration.

T1078.name: Valid Accounts
T1078.description: Adversaries obtain and abuse the credentials of existing accounts (default, local, domain, or cloud) to gain initial access, persist, escalate privileges, and evade detection by appearing as legitimate users.
T1078.detection: Monitor for logons from unusual sources, times, or geolocations; impossible-travel; use of dormant/service accounts; and privileged access outside normal patterns. Correlate authentication with behavioral baselines.
T1078.mitigation: Enforce MFA, disable or rename default accounts, apply least privilege, rotate credentials, and monitor for anomalous account usage.

T1080.name: Taint Shared Content
T1080.description: Adversaries add malicious content or modify legitimate files on shared storage (network shares, code repositories) so that other users execute the payload when accessing it.
T1080.detection: Monitor writes to shared executables, scripts, and document repositories, and flag modifications to files on shares by unexpected accounts.
T1080.mitigation: Least-privilege share permissions, integrity monitoring of shared content, and application control limit tainting and its execution.

T1082.name: System Information Discovery
T1082.description: Adversaries gather detailed information about a host — OS version, hardware, hostname, patches, architecture — to tailor further actions.
T1082.detection: Monitor for execution of systeminfo, hostname, uname, wmic, and reg query commands, particularly scripted or in bursts with other discovery activity.
T1082.mitigation: Built-in system information gathering is difficult to prevent; focus on detecting anomalous or automated enumeration.

T1083.name: File and Directory Discovery
T1083.description: Adversaries enumerate files and directories on a host or network share to find data of interest and understand the file-system layout.
T1083.detection: Look for scripted or rapid directory traversal (dir, ls, tree, find, Get-ChildItem) especially targeting user, document, or credential-bearing paths.
T1083.mitigation: This discovery of built-in file listings is hard to prevent; concentrate on detection and least-privilege access to sensitive directories.

T1087.name: Account Discovery
T1087.description: Adversaries enumerate local, domain, cloud, or email accounts to understand available identities and target them for access or privilege escalation.
T1087.detection: Monitor for account-enumeration commands (net user/net group, dsquery, ldapsearch, Get-ADUser) and unusual LDAP/directory queries from non-admin hosts.
T1087.mitigation: Restrict and audit directory enumeration where possible, apply least privilege, and detect anomalous LDAP/AD queries.

T1090.name: Proxy
T1090.description: Adversaries route traffic through intermediary proxy systems — internal hops, external proxies, or multi-hop chains — to obscure the true source of C2 and evade attribution.
T1090.detection: Look for internal hosts acting as relays, connections to known proxy/anonymizer infrastructure (e.g. Tor), and unexpected internal-to-internal forwarding.
T1090.mitigation: Network intrusion prevention, egress filtering, and blocking known anonymization services reduce proxied C2.

T1091.name: Replication Through Removable Media
T1091.description: Adversaries move onto systems (including air-gapped ones) by copying malware to removable media and relying on autorun or user execution when the media is used elsewhere.
T1091.detection: Monitor for executable/script writes to removable drives, process execution originating from removable media, and autorun artifacts.
T1091.mitigation: Disable autorun/autoplay, restrict or block removable media use via device control, and scan media on connection.

T1095.name: Non-Application Layer Protocol
T1095.description: Adversaries use non-application-layer protocols (ICMP, raw TCP/UDP, or other OSI layer 3/4 protocols) for command and control to avoid inspection focused on application traffic.
T1095.detection: Flag anomalous ICMP volumes/payloads, unexpected raw socket traffic, and use of uncommon protocols between hosts.
T1095.mitigation: Restrict allowed protocols with firewalls, apply network intrusion prevention, and filter unnecessary protocols at egress.

T1098.name: Account Manipulation
T1098.description: Adversaries modify accounts — changing credentials, adding permissions or group memberships, or attaching credentials — to maintain or elevate access.
T1098.detection: Monitor directory and IAM change events: group membership additions (especially privileged groups), permission grants, credential/SSH-key additions, and MFA changes.
T1098.mitigation: Enforce least privilege and MFA, alert on privileged group changes, and require multi-party approval for sensitive account modifications.

T1105.name: Ingress Tool Transfer
T1105.description: Adversaries transfer tools, malware, or other files from an external system into a compromised environment to support their operations.
T1105.detection: Look for downloads by living-off-the-land utilities (certutil, bitsadmin, curl, wget, PowerShell DownloadFile), and file writes shortly followed by execution.
T1105.mitigation: Egress filtering, application control on downloaded binaries, and network intrusion prevention limit tool transfer.

T1106.name: Native API
T1106.description: Adversaries invoke operating-system native APIs directly to execute code and interact with the system, bypassing higher-level utilities that are more heavily monitored.
T1106.detection: Behavioral detection of suspicious API call sequences (e.g. process creation, memory allocation, injection primitives) and unusual direct syscalls; leverage EDR telemetry.
T1106.mitigation: Application control, exploit protection, and behavioral EDR are the primary controls; the API surface itself cannot be disabled.

T1110.name: Brute Force
T1110.description: Adversaries guess credentials through repeated attempts — password guessing, password spraying, credential stuffing, or offline hash cracking — to obtain valid accounts.
T1110.detection: Alert on many failed authentications for one account (guessing), one attempt across many accounts (spraying), and failures followed by success. Correlate by source IP and account.
T1110.mitigation: Enforce account lockout/throttling, strong password policy, MFA, and monitoring for spray/stuffing patterns.

T1112.name: Modify Registry
T1112.description: Adversaries create, modify, or delete Windows Registry keys and values to configure persistence, alter system behavior, store data, or evade defenses.
T1112.detection: Monitor registry writes to autostart, service, and security-relevant keys (Run keys, services, Winlogon), and use of reg.exe/regedit from unusual processes.
T1112.mitigation: Restrict write access to sensitive registry keys with least privilege and use application control and integrity monitoring.

T1114.name: Email Collection
T1114.description: Adversaries collect email — from local mail stores, remote mailboxes, or via forwarding rules — to obtain sensitive information and communications.
T1114.detection: Monitor for creation of mail-forwarding rules, bulk mailbox access/export, access to PST/OST files, and unusual mailbox API activity.
T1114.mitigation: Audit and restrict auto-forwarding rules, apply MFA and least privilege to mailboxes, and alert on bulk export.

T1133.name: External Remote Services
T1133.description: Adversaries use externally-facing remote services (VPN, RDP gateways, Citrix, SSH) with valid accounts to gain initial access or persist in an environment.
T1133.detection: Monitor VPN/remote-gateway authentication for unusual sources, times, or accounts; flag first-time external logons and impossible travel.
T1133.mitigation: Require MFA on all external remote access, restrict access with allowlists, disable unused services, and place gateways behind segmentation.

T1134.name: Access Token Manipulation
T1134.description: Adversaries manipulate Windows access tokens — duplicating, impersonating, or creating tokens — to run actions under a different user's security context and escalate or evade.
T1134.detection: Monitor for token-manipulation API usage (e.g. DuplicateTokenEx, ImpersonateLoggedOnUser), unexpected privilege changes, and processes running as unexpected users.
T1134.mitigation: Apply least privilege, restrict assignment of sensitive privileges (SeDebugPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege), and use EDR behavioral detection.

T1135.name: Network Share Discovery
T1135.description: Adversaries enumerate shared drives and folders on remote systems to locate data and identify targets for lateral movement or collection.
T1135.detection: Monitor for share-enumeration commands (net view, net share, Get-SmbShare) and SMB session enumeration, especially from non-admin hosts in bursts.
T1135.mitigation: Least-privilege share permissions, disabling unnecessary shares, and detecting enumeration reduce exposure.

T1136.name: Create Account
T1136.description: Adversaries create local, domain, or cloud accounts to establish persistent access under credentials they control.
T1136.detection: Alert on account-creation events, especially new privileged or service accounts created outside normal provisioning workflows or by unexpected actors.
T1136.mitigation: Restrict account-creation rights, require approval workflows, and monitor and alert on all new-account events.

T1189.name: Drive-by Compromise
T1189.description: Adversaries gain access when a user visits a malicious or compromised website that exploits the browser or its plugins to execute code without further user action.
T1189.detection: Look for browser processes spawning shells or writing/executing files, exploit-kit indicators, and connections to malicious/low-reputation web infrastructure.
T1189.mitigation: Keep browsers and plugins patched, use exploit protection and sandboxing, block malicious sites, and restrict script/plugin execution.

T1190.name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
T1190.description: Adversaries exploit vulnerabilities in internet-facing applications or services (web apps, APIs, databases, network appliances) to gain initial access.
T1190.detection: Monitor web/app server logs for exploitation patterns (injection, deserialization, path traversal), web servers spawning shells, and post-exploitation file writes to web roots.
T1190.mitigation: Patch promptly, use a web application firewall, apply least privilege and segmentation for exposed services, and remove unnecessary internet exposure.

T1195.name: Supply Chain Compromise
T1195.description: Adversaries compromise a product, dependency, update mechanism, or vendor before delivery so that trusted software or hardware carries malicious code to victims.
T1195.detection: Detect via anomalous behavior of trusted software post-update, integrity/hash mismatches of dependencies and updates, and unexpected outbound connections from vendor software.
T1195.mitigation: Verify integrity and signatures of software/updates, vet third-party dependencies, and use least privilege plus behavioral monitoring for trusted software.

T1199.name: Trusted Relationship
T1199.description: Adversaries abuse an existing trusted relationship — such as a managed service provider, contractor, or partner connection — to access a victim through that trusted third party.
T1199.detection: Monitor access originating from partner/MSP connections for anomalies, and treat third-party access with the same scrutiny (and logging) as internal privileged access.
T1199.mitigation: Apply least privilege and network segmentation to third-party connections, require MFA, and audit external partner access.

T1203.name: Exploitation for Client Execution
T1203.description: Adversaries exploit vulnerabilities in client applications (browsers, office suites, PDF readers) so that opening a crafted document or file executes code.
T1203.detection: Look for client applications (Office, Acrobat, browsers) spawning script interpreters or writing/executing payloads, and known exploit indicators.
T1203.mitigation: Patch client software, disable macros and risky features, use exploit protection and application sandboxing, and block risky file types.

T1204.name: User Execution
T1204.description: Adversaries rely on a user to open a malicious file or link (e.g. from phishing) to trigger execution of the payload.
T1204.detection: Correlate email/download delivery with subsequent execution: document or archive processes spawning interpreters, and users running unexpected downloaded binaries.
T1204.mitigation: User security awareness training, blocking risky attachments, disabling macros, and application control reduce successful user execution.

T1218.name: System Binary Proxy Execution
T1218.description: Adversaries proxy execution of malicious code through trusted, signed system binaries (LOLBins such as rundll32, regsvr32, mshta, msiexec) to bypass application control and evade detection.
T1218.detection: Monitor for signed system utilities executing remote or unusual payloads, atypical command-line arguments, and LOLBins making network connections.
T1218.mitigation: Application control rules that constrain how LOLBins can be used, plus behavioral EDR detection, mitigate proxy execution.

T1222.name: File and Directory Permissions Modification
T1222.description: Adversaries modify file or directory permissions/ownership (ACLs, chmod/chown, icacls) to enable access, evade defenses, or make malicious files usable or hidden.
T1222.detection: Monitor for permission-changing commands (icacls, cacls, chmod, chown, takeown) on system or security-relevant paths, particularly from non-admin processes.
T1222.mitigation: Apply least privilege, restrict who can change permissions on sensitive paths, and use integrity monitoring/auditing of ACL changes.

T1485.name: Data Destruction
T1485.description: Adversaries destroy data and files on systems — overwriting, deleting, or corrupting them — to disrupt operations and impair availability, sometimes irrecoverably.
T1485.detection: Look for mass file deletion/overwrite, use of secure-delete or disk-wiping tools, and rapid destructive I/O across many files.
T1485.mitigation: Maintain tested, offline/immutable backups, restrict destructive permissions with least privilege, and alert on mass deletion.

T1486.name: Data Encrypted for Impact
T1486.description: Adversaries encrypt data on target systems (ransomware) to deny availability and extort the victim, often across many files and hosts simultaneously.
T1486.detection: Detect rapid mass file modification with rising entropy, ransom-note creation, shadow-copy deletion, and known ransomware tool/behavior signatures.
T1486.mitigation: Maintain tested offline/immutable backups, use behavioral anti-ransomware controls, segment networks to limit spread, and apply least privilege.

T1489.name: Service Stop
T1489.description: Adversaries stop or disable services — often security, database, or backup services — to disrupt availability or to remove obstacles before further impact.
T1489.detection: Monitor for service stop/disable commands (sc stop, net stop, systemctl stop) and Service Control Manager events, especially targeting security or backup services.
T1489.mitigation: Restrict permissions to stop critical services, protect security-service processes with tamper protection, and alert on stop/disable events.

T1490.name: Inhibit System Recovery
T1490.description: Adversaries delete or disable recovery features — Volume Shadow Copies, backup catalogs, boot recovery — to prevent restoration after a destructive or ransomware attack.
T1490.detection: Alert on vssadmin/wbadmin delete, bcdedit recovery changes, shadow-copy deletion, and disabling of backup services — classic pre-encryption ransomware behavior.
T1490.mitigation: Keep offline/immutable backups outside the host's control, restrict permissions to recovery tooling, and alert on recovery-deletion commands.

T1491.name: Defacement
T1491.description: Adversaries modify visual content — websites, internal pages, or host displays — to deliver a message, intimidate, or claim credit, damaging integrity and reputation.
T1491.detection: Monitor for unexpected changes to web root/content files, integrity-check failures on served pages, and unusual writes by web-server processes.
T1491.mitigation: Integrity monitoring of web content, least-privilege write access to web roots, patching of web apps, and tested backups for rapid restoration.

T1499.name: Endpoint Denial of Service
T1499.description: Adversaries exhaust the resources of a specific endpoint or service (CPU, memory, connections, application logic) to make it unavailable to legitimate users.
T1499.detection: Look for resource-exhaustion patterns — sustained high CPU/memory, connection floods, or application error spikes — on targeted hosts and services.
T1499.mitigation: Rate limiting, resource quotas, load balancing, upstream DoS protection, and patching of resource-exhaustion vulnerabilities.

T1505.name: Server Software Component
T1505.description: Adversaries abuse extensibility of server software — web shells, malicious IIS/Apache modules, SQL stored procedures, transport agents — to persist and execute code on servers.
T1505.detection: Monitor for new/modified files in web roots (web shells), unexpected server modules/extensions, web servers spawning shells, and anomalous server-component loads.
T1505.mitigation: Restrict write access to server content and module directories, apply least privilege, use file integrity monitoring, and patch server software.

T1537.name: Transfer Data to Cloud Account
T1537.description: Adversaries exfiltrate by transferring data to a cloud account or storage they control (e.g. copying to an attacker-owned bucket or sharing snapshots), keeping traffic within cloud-provider networks.
T1537.detection: Monitor cloud audit logs for data copied/shared to external accounts, snapshot sharing, and unusual bulk object transfers to unfamiliar destinations.
T1537.mitigation: Restrict cross-account sharing with policy, monitor cloud data-transfer events, and apply least privilege to cloud storage.

T1539.name: Steal Web Session Cookie
T1539.description: Adversaries steal web session cookies or tokens to hijack authenticated sessions and bypass credential authentication, including MFA.
T1539.detection: Look for access to browser cookie stores, session reuse from new devices/IPs (impossible travel), and token use inconsistent with the originating device.
T1539.mitigation: Bind sessions to device/context, shorten session lifetimes, protect cookie stores, and use phishing-resistant MFA and token-binding where available.

T1543.name: Create or Modify System Process
T1543.description: Adversaries create or modify system-level processes — Windows services, Linux systemd services, launch daemons/agents — to execute malicious code, usually for persistence and elevated privilege.
T1543.detection: Monitor for new/modified services and daemons (Service Control Manager events, systemd unit changes, sc/systemctl usage) pointing to unusual binaries or paths.
T1543.mitigation: Restrict permissions to create/modify services, apply least privilege, and audit service/daemon changes with integrity monitoring.

T1547.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
T1547.description: Adversaries configure the system to automatically execute their code at boot or logon — via Run keys, startup folders, kernel modules, or similar autostart mechanisms — for persistence.
T1547.detection: Monitor autostart locations (Run keys, startup folders, services, LSA/Winlogon, kernel modules) for new or modified entries pointing to suspicious binaries.
T1547.mitigation: Restrict write access to autostart locations, use application control, and audit autostart-configuration changes.

T1548.name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
T1548.description: Adversaries bypass or abuse privilege-elevation controls — UAC bypass, sudo/setuid abuse, elevated-prompt manipulation — to obtain higher privileges.
T1548.detection: Monitor for known UAC-bypass techniques, unexpected auto-elevating processes, setuid/sudo misuse, and privilege elevation without corresponding prompts.
T1548.mitigation: Configure UAC to the highest practical level, restrict sudo/setuid, apply least privilege, and remove local admin where unnecessary.

T1550.name: Use Alternate Authentication Material
T1550.description: Adversaries authenticate using stolen material other than a plaintext password — password hashes (pass-the-hash), Kerberos tickets (pass-the-ticket), or application access tokens — to move laterally without knowing the password.
T1550.detection: Detect pass-the-hash/ticket via logon-type and NTLM/Kerberos anomalies, tickets used from unexpected hosts, and token reuse inconsistent with normal patterns.
T1550.mitigation: Enable Credential Guard, restrict privileged-account reuse across hosts, rotate krbtgt, enforce MFA, and segment administrative tiers.

T1552.name: Unsecured Credentials
T1552.description: Adversaries search for credentials stored insecurely — in files, scripts, configuration, environment variables, registry, cloud metadata, or command history — and reuse them.
T1552.detection: Monitor for access to credential-bearing files (config, .env, private keys), cloud instance-metadata requests, and searches through history/registry for password patterns.
T1552.mitigation: Use secret managers/vaults, remove hardcoded credentials, restrict access to credential stores, and rotate exposed secrets.

T1555.name: Credentials from Password Stores
T1555.description: Adversaries extract credentials from password stores such as browser password managers, keychains, the Windows Credential Manager, and third-party vaults.
T1555.detection: Monitor for access to browser/OS credential stores and keychain files, and use of tools that dump saved credentials.
T1555.mitigation: Protect and encrypt password stores, apply least privilege, use MFA to reduce reuse value, and restrict access to keychain/credential files.

T1558.name: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets
T1558.description: Adversaries abuse Kerberos — Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, golden/silver ticket forgery — to obtain or forge tickets granting access to services and domains.
T1558.detection: Detect anomalous TGS requests (Kerberoasting), RC4-encrypted ticket requests, tickets with abnormal lifetimes, and service-ticket requests for many SPNs.
T1558.mitigation: Use strong service-account passwords/gMSA, restrict SPNs, enforce AES encryption, rotate krbtgt regularly, and monitor Kerberos activity.

T1560.name: Archive Collected Data
T1560.description: Adversaries compress and/or encrypt collected data into archives before exfiltration to reduce size and evade content inspection.
T1560.detection: Look for archive-utility execution (zip, rar, 7z, tar) and archive creation shortly before outbound transfers, and password-protected archives in staging areas.
T1560.mitigation: Data loss prevention, monitoring for archive creation in unusual contexts, and inspecting/blocking large outbound transfers.

T1562.name: Impair Defenses
T1562.description: Adversaries disable, modify, or interfere with security controls — antivirus/EDR, firewalls, logging, and event monitoring — to avoid detection and enable further activity.
T1562.detection: Alert on stopping/disabling of security services, log-source silence, tampering with AV/EDR configuration, and firewall-rule changes weakening protection.
T1562.mitigation: Enable tamper protection on security tools, restrict permissions to modify them, forward logs off-host, and alert when a log source goes silent.

T1566.name: Phishing
T1566.description: Adversaries send phishing messages — malicious attachments, links, or social engineering — to trick users into executing payloads or divulging credentials, a common initial-access vector.
T1566.detection: Inspect email for malicious attachments/links and spoofing, correlate delivery with subsequent user execution or credential entry, and detect credential-harvesting pages.
T1566.mitigation: Email filtering and attachment sandboxing, link rewriting, user awareness training, and MFA to blunt credential theft.

T1567.name: Exfiltration Over Web Service
T1567.description: Adversaries exfiltrate data to legitimate external web services (cloud storage, code-sharing, paste sites) whose traffic is commonly allowed, blending with normal usage.
T1567.detection: Monitor for uploads to cloud-storage/paste/code services from unusual hosts or in unusual volumes, and connections to such services by server or service accounts.
T1567.mitigation: Restrict or allowlist outbound access to web services, use data loss prevention and CASB controls, and inspect large outbound transfers.

T1569.name: System Services
T1569.description: Adversaries execute code by abusing the OS service manager — creating or manipulating services (e.g. via sc.exe, service execution) to run commands, sometimes with elevated privileges.
T1569.detection: Monitor for service creation and execution events, sc.exe/PsExec service usage, and services launching cmd/PowerShell or binaries from unusual paths.
T1569.mitigation: Restrict who can create/start services, apply least privilege, and audit service-creation and execution events.

T1570.name: Lateral Tool Transfer
T1570.description: Adversaries copy tools and files between internal systems (via SMB shares, admin shares, or remote copy) to stage capabilities as they move laterally.
T1570.detection: Look for file writes to remote admin shares (ADMIN$, C$), internal SMB transfers of executables/scripts, and copies shortly followed by remote execution.
T1570.mitigation: Restrict admin-share access, segment the network, apply least privilege, and monitor internal file-transfer patterns.

T1571.name: Non-Standard Port
T1571.description: Adversaries run command-and-control or exfiltration protocols over non-standard ports (e.g. HTTPS on an unusual port) to evade port-based filtering and detection.
T1571.detection: Detect protocol/port mismatches — a known protocol on an unexpected port — and outbound connections to uncommon ports from server or user hosts.
T1571.mitigation: Egress filtering that constrains destination ports, protocol-aware inspection, and network intrusion prevention reduce non-standard-port C2.

T1572.name: Protocol Tunneling
T1572.description: Adversaries tunnel their traffic inside another protocol (e.g. C2 over DNS, HTTP, or SSH tunnels) to conceal it and bypass network controls.
T1572.detection: Look for tunneling indicators — long/high-entropy DNS queries, unexpected SSH tunnels, and payloads inconsistent with the carrier protocol.
T1572.mitigation: Network intrusion prevention, DNS monitoring, egress filtering, and blocking unauthorized tunneling utilities limit covert tunnels.

T1573.name: Encrypted Channel
T1573.description: Adversaries encrypt C2 traffic using standard TLS or custom/symmetric cryptography to hide command-and-control content from inspection.
T1573.detection: Where payload inspection is blocked by encryption, rely on metadata: beaconing cadence, JA3/TLS fingerprints, self-signed or anomalous certificates, and destination reputation.
T1573.mitigation: TLS inspection where permitted, network intrusion prevention, and blocking connections to low-reputation destinations.

T1583.name: Acquire Infrastructure
T1583.description: Adversaries buy, lease, or register infrastructure — domains, servers, VPS, cloud accounts — ahead of an operation to support C2, delivery, and staging. (Pre-compromise; rarely visible in victim telemetry.)
T1583.detection: Largely detected via external threat intelligence on newly-registered domains and known-bad infrastructure rather than on-host signals; correlate alerts with such indicators.
T1583.mitigation: This pre-attack activity occurs outside victim environments and cannot be directly mitigated; rely on threat-intel blocking of known adversary infrastructure.

T1584.name: Compromise Infrastructure
T1584.description: Adversaries compromise third-party infrastructure (servers, domains, network devices, cloud accounts) belonging to others and repurpose it for their operations. (Pre-compromise from the victim's view.)
T1584.detection: Detected chiefly through threat intelligence on compromised/abused infrastructure; correlate C2 or delivery indicators against such reputation data.
T1584.mitigation: Occurs outside the victim environment; mitigation relies on threat-intel-driven blocking of known-abused infrastructure.

T1586.name: Compromise Accounts
T1586.description: Adversaries compromise existing third-party accounts (email, social media, cloud, developer) to use in later operations such as phishing or infrastructure access. (Pre-compromise resource development.)
T1586.detection: Not typically visible in victim on-host telemetry; surfaced via threat intelligence about compromised sender/service accounts used against the organization.
T1586.mitigation: External to the victim environment; rely on threat intelligence and on verifying the identity of external senders and services.

T1587.name: Develop Capabilities
T1587.description: Adversaries build their own capabilities — malware, exploits, certificates, or tooling — to support operations rather than obtaining them from others. (Pre-compromise resource development.)
T1587.detection: Not observable in victim telemetry; relevant mainly as context via threat intelligence about a group's tooling and TTPs.
T1587.mitigation: This development occurs outside victim environments and cannot be directly mitigated; focus on detection of the resulting capabilities in use.

T1588.name: Obtain Capabilities
T1588.description: Adversaries obtain capabilities — malware, exploits, code-signing certificates, tools, or vulnerability information — from third parties for use in operations. (Pre-compromise resource development.)
T1588.detection: Not directly observable in victim telemetry; informs detection via threat intelligence on tooling, certificates, and exploits an adversary is known to use.
T1588.mitigation: Occurs outside the victim environment; mitigation relies on detecting the obtained capabilities when they are deployed.

T1589.name: Gather Victim Identity Information
T1589.description: Adversaries collect identity information about the target — email addresses, employee names, credentials — through open sources or active means to support later targeting. (Reconnaissance.)
T1589.detection: Mostly conducted outside victim visibility; some active collection may appear as phishing for information or unusual queries against public-facing identity services.
T1589.mitigation: Limit exposure of employee/identity details publicly and train staff; much of this reconnaissance cannot be directly prevented.

T1590.name: Gather Victim Network Information
T1590.description: Adversaries collect information about the target's networks — IP ranges, domains, topology, DNS — via open sources or active scanning to plan operations. (Reconnaissance.)
T1590.detection: Active elements may surface as external scanning or unusual DNS/WHOIS queries against the organization; much passive collection is not visible.
T1590.mitigation: Minimize exposed network information and monitor external-facing services; passive OSINT collection largely cannot be prevented.

T1591.name: Gather Victim Org Information
T1591.description: Adversaries collect organizational details — business structure, roles, physical locations, relationships — to inform targeting and social engineering. (Reconnaissance.)
T1591.detection: Largely conducted through open sources outside victim telemetry; occasionally visible as social-engineering probes or pretexting.
T1591.mitigation: Limit sensitive organizational detail exposed publicly and train staff against pretexting; passive collection cannot be directly prevented.

T1592.name: Gather Victim Host Information
T1592.description: Adversaries collect information about target hosts — hardware, software, OS, configuration — via open sources or active probing to tailor later actions. (Reconnaissance.)
T1592.detection: Active probing (banner grabbing, fingerprinting) may appear in external service logs; much passive collection is not visible to the victim.
T1592.mitigation: Minimize information leaked by public-facing services (banners, error pages); passive host-information gathering cannot be fully prevented.

T1595.name: Active Scanning
T1595.description: Adversaries actively probe the target's infrastructure — scanning IP blocks, ports, and vulnerabilities — to identify reachable services and weaknesses. (Reconnaissance.)
T1595.detection: Detect port/vulnerability scanning against external-facing assets via firewall, IDS, and web-server logs: many connections across ports/hosts from one source in a short window.
T1595.mitigation: Reduce external attack surface, use intrusion prevention on internet-facing assets, and monitor for scanning; the activity itself originates externally.
